Twelve Beautiful Lies

Why are there twelve notes? Why is every one of them slightly out of tune? Same answer. The spiral above is discovering it on loop.

Scroll down to read ↓

Part two established that the consonant intervals — octave 2:1, fifth 3:2, third 5:4 — are physical facts, valleys in a roughness landscape your cochlea computes for free. The trap: try to build an instrument around those ratios. A flute is a row of holes; a piano is a row of strings; a fretboard is a row of metal bars. All of them demand that you choose, in advance and forever, a finite set of frequencies. Which ones?

The obvious plan: start from a home note and generate new notes using the purest intervals. Octaves alone give you nothing new — 2:1 is just the same note higher, the runner-up — so use the next-best interval, the fifth. Multiply by 3:2, fold the result back into one octave (divide by two as needed — by octave equivalence it's the "same" note), and repeat. Each step adds a fresh note. Do it twelve times and the sequence almost returns to its starting point.

Step 1 · Stacked fifthsA spiral that won't close

Stack the fifths below one at a time. The angle around the clock is the note's position inside the octave (its pitch class); the radius just counts how many fifths deep you are. Listen as you go: C, G, D, A, E… each new note lands on an unclaimed spoke of the clock. After twelve steps you have generated exactly twelve distinct notes — the chromatic scale assembling itself out of one ratio — and the thirteenth note lands back on C. The system closes. Twelve notes, generated by the fifth.

Except it doesn't close. Look at the red arc.

Twelve pure fifths overshoot seven octaves by a stubborn sliver: 

(3/2)¹² = 129.746…        2⁷ = 128

129.746 / 128 = 1.01364…  →  23.46 cents sharp  (a "cent" = 1/100 of a semitone)

That sliver is the Pythagorean comma, and it is not a rounding error you can engineer away — it's number theory. Powers of 3/2 are never powers of 2; the spiral of fifths is a true spiral, forever, and every tuning system in history is some strategy for hiding that fact. Now drag the temper slider. Shaving each fifth by just under two cents — an error far below what anyone can hear melodically — closes the spiral into a perfect circle. The resulting approximation makes a finite keyboard possible. It is called equal temperament, and it is what modern pianos, guitar frets, and most synthesizers use.

Aside · 23.46¢ of history

Between "pure fifths" and "twelve equal lies" lie centuries of intermediate schemes. Pythagorean tuning kept eleven fifths pure and dumped the entire comma into one unusable interval — the howling wolf fifth, which organists simply avoided. Meantone temperament (Renaissance standard) sacrificed the fifths further to buy sweet pure thirds, but only in nearby keys — stray too far and the wolf returned. The "well temperaments" of Bach's era (Werckmeister's among them) spread the comma unevenly so that every key worked but each kept its own flavour — likely what The Well-Tempered Clavier was actually written for, not equal temperament. The full equal split won only in the 19th century, when pianos and orchestras made constant modulation the norm.

Step 2 · What the lie costsHearing the comma

Two cents per fifth is free — no ear catches it. But part two taught us where tuning errors actually hurt: not in melodies but in handshakes, the coinciding harmonics of simultaneous notes, where any mismatch turns into beating. So the real question is: what do equal temperament's approximations do to chords?

The fifth survives beautifully — 700¢ versus the pure 701.955, and the beat between the colliding partials is under one per second; you'd need lab conditions to care. The casualty is the major third. Equal temperament's third is 400¢ against a pure 386.3¢ — 13.7 cents sharp, seven times the fifth's error. On a C-major chord, the harmonics that should coincide miss by about ten hertz, and the chord acquires a permanent, gentle shimmer. Compare for yourself; the lights between the strings pulse at each pair's true beat rate.

Alternate a few times and then listen to any sustained pop-song synth pad: you will start hearing the shimmer everywhere, because it is everywhere. Major thirds on an equal-tempered keyboard beat like that. Most listeners accept it as part of the instrument's sound. (Barbershop quartets, string quartets, and choirs, unchained from frets and keys, drift toward the pure ratios the moment they sustain a chord — singers call it "locking in", and it's the left panel they're finding.)

Deep dive: cents — the logarithm music runs on

The cent, introduced by Alexander Ellis in his 1885 translation of Helmholtz, is the decibel of pitch: 1200·log₂(ratio). It exists because the ear hears ratios (part one), so equal perceptual steps must be equal multiplications — and comparing tuning systems in raw hertz is meaningless. In cents, equal temperament is the definition of roundness: every semitone exactly 100. The pure intervals become the crooked ones: fifth 701.955, major third 386.314, minor third 315.641. Tuning theory is the art of rounding those numbers — and this entire post is just the story of rounding 701.955 to 700.

Step 3 · Why twelve and not thirteen?An accident of arithmetic

One question remains. Equal division was forced on us — but equal division into twelve? Why not ten (we have fingers), or some other count? The answer has nothing to do with biology or culture. Divide the octave into N equal steps and ask of each N: how close does its nearest step come to the pure fifth? That's the entire criterion — and N = 12 is unusually good.

Click the columns and listen — each plays the true 3:2 fifth, then that system's best imitation. Five and seven steps (real scales in world musics) miss by an audible 15–20 cents. Most counts in between do no better. Then twelve lands within two cents, and to beat it you must climb to 29, 41, or 53 steps. The mathematical root: log₂(3/2) = 0.58496…, and the continued-fraction expansion of that number serves up 7/12 as an absurdly good approximation for so small a denominator. Twelve notes is not a law of physics; it's a lucky truncation.

Aside · The systems that took the other fork

The chart's other heroes had real careers. 19-TET was seriously proposed in the Renaissance (Guillaume Costeley, Francisco de Salinas) because its third is sweeter than ours. 31-TET — championed by Christiaan Huygens, the man who explained Saturn's rings — nearly nails the pure third, and actual 31-tone organs were built in the Netherlands and are still played. 53-TET was noted by Isaac Newton in his student notebooks and remains the theoretical backbone of Turkish makam music. And many traditions never wanted equal steps at all: Indonesian gamelan tunings (recall part two — their inharmonic bell timbres move the consonance valleys) and the quarter-tone-rich maqam world both thrive off this chart entirely.

EpilogueThe twelve-note grid

Tally the journey. Physics handed us perfect intervals (part two); number theory refused to let them tile an octave; and the engineering compromise — twelve equal steps, each fifth shaved 1.955¢, each major third inflated 13.7¢ — bought us something no pure tuning could ever offer: every key works, and they all work identically. You can start a melody on any of the twelve notes and get the exact same shape. You can drift from C major to F♯ major mid-song without retuning the orchestra. The result is a uniform twelve-note grid.

Everything from here on in this series happens on that grid. And the very next thing music does with those twelve notes is avoid using them all at once: nearly every melody you know lives inside a hand-picked seven of the twelve. Which seven, why seven, what happens when you pick a different seven — and why the answer is best drawn as a rotating ring — is part four.

Continued in Part four: Seven of Twelve — scales as step patterns, modes as rotations of the same ring, and the circle of fifths reborn as a map of keys.

← Part two: Why Notes Agree · Part four: Seven of Twelve →

Sound by Tone.js. Standing on the shoulders of: Ellis (1885) · Werckmeister (1691) · the comma the Greeks already knew
